prompt: Hyperrealistic cinematic photograph, cold steel-blue and white tones, shot with extreme deep focus. A single figure stands in the foreground center of an infinite mirrored corridor. The figure faces away from the viewer. The figure is entirely alone in the real space. On both sides, floor-to-ceiling mirrors reflect not the empty corridor — but dense, interconnected crowds of people. Each person in the crowd is visibly turned toward someone else, engaged with neighbors, forming tight clusters of connection. No one in the crowd is looking at the central figure. Critically: within the crowd reflections, if you look closely at any single individual in the crowd, that individual also appears slightly more peripheral than the people around them — every person in the crowd is subtly at the edge of a denser cluster. This recursive isolation is visible at every level of the image. As the mirrors recede into infinite depth, the crowds become denser and more interconnected, while the lone central figure's reflections become smaller and more isolated. The vanishing point shows an infinitely dense social world that the central figure is always just outside of. The lighting is cool, clinical, slightly melancholic. The floor is polished reflective steel. No warmth. No color except blue-white-silver. The composition is perfectly symmetrical left to right. The figure's posture suggests not sadness but calm mathematical certainty — as if this is simply how geometry works, not a tragedy. The crowd figures are slightly soft-focus and impressionistic — their density and interconnection is clear but individual faces are not sharply defined. No text. No numbers. No labels. No charts. No symbols. No warm colors. No eye contact with viewer. Style: architectural photography meets psychological thriller concept art. Shot quality of a Gregory Crewdson photograph. Aspect ratio 16:9, minimum 1792px wide.
visual_logic: A lone figure surrounded by mirrors showing dense, interconnected crowds — yet within those crowds, each individual is also subtly peripheral to denser clusters around them. Isolation is recursive and universal: everyone is simultaneously the least-connected node.
